skill.name: cascade-orchestrator
skill.description: Designs workflow cascades that coordinate micro-skills across sequential, parallel, conditional, Codex sandbox, multi-model, and swarm stages.
skill.summary: Coordinate multi-skill AI workflows with model routing, sandbox iteration, memory, and GitHub handoffs.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external-command and weak-crypto patterns, but review found these are Markdown examples and substring matches, not executable code. The skill is documentation-only and shows no malicious intent or prompt injection, but it describes autonomous Codex, GitHub, memory, and swarm workflows that can affect external systems when followed.

medium_findings.title: Agentic Workflow Orchestration Requires Guardrails
medium_findings.description: The skill describes Codex sandbox iteration, GitHub automation, ruv-swarm coordination, and persistent memory. This is not malicious, but workflows built from these instructions could modify files, create pull requests, or retain context if users grant those tools access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced sections explicitly describe external integrations and autonomous repair behavior. The risk depends on user-granted tool permissions, so this is a moderate operational concern rather than confirmed malware.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external-command findings correspond to fenced YAML, Python, and Markdown examples. I found no Ruby backtick execution or runnable shell script in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are documentation code blocks that demonstrate cascade definitions and pseudocode. They are not executable files and do not invoke a shell.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear on ordinary words such as description, Design, and cascades. No evidence found of DES, MD5, SHA1, encryption APIs, keys, or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line context is prose or YAML field names, not cryptographic code. The scanner likely matched short substrings inside normal words.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Task Descriptions
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts occur in workflow example text about research and prototyping. No evidence found of host inventory commands, environment dumping, network probing, or system enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are natural-language workflow requirements, not command invocations. They do not request system data or enumerate the host environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Autonomous Auto-Fix Workflow Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends iterative Codex repair loops with repeated tests and optional application of fixes. This pattern should run only in an isolated sandbox with explicit review before changes reach the main workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly describes auto-fix loops and sandbox settings. It is a legitimate automation pattern, but the impact can be significant if sandbox or review controls are disabled.

content.value_statement: Complex AI work often spans many tools, models, and review steps. This skill helps design structured cascades that route tasks through micro-skills, Codex, Claude, swarm workers, and GitHub handoffs.
actual_capabilities: Defines sequential, parallel, conditional, Codex sandbox, multi-model, and swarm workflow stages.
Shows how to route stages to Claude, Codex, Gemini, or swarm execution based on task needs.
Provides cascade patterns for quality checks, legacy modernization, bug fixes, and development workflows.
Documents retry, fallback, model switching, and Codex auto-fix error handling strategies.
Includes examples for shared memory, GitHub pull request handoffs, and stage-level inputs and outputs.
limitations: It provides orchestration guidance, not a runnable workflow engine by itself.
External tools such as Codex, Gemini, GitHub, and ruv-swarm must already be available.
Sandbox safety depends on the user enforcing isolation, permissions, and human review.
The examples are high-level patterns and may need adaptation for each repository.

use_cases.title: Plan a Multi-Stage Engineering Workflow
use_cases.description: Create a cascade that moves from research to implementation, testing, review, and pull request creation.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Parallel Quality Checks
use_cases.description: Split linting, security review, complexity analysis, and test coverage into coordinated parallel stages.

use_cases.title: Design Controlled Auto-Fix Loops
use_cases.description: Add Codex sandbox stages that rerun tests, propose fixes, and escalate failures after a clear limit.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Cascade
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a cascade for my goal: [goal]. Use these micro-skills: [skills]. Keep the stages simple and explain each handoff.

prompt_templates.title: Add Parallel Quality Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a cascade for [project] with parallel quality checks for linting, tests, security, and documentation. Include aggregation and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Route Work Across Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a cascade that routes each stage to Claude, Codex, Gemini, or swarm workers based on context size, speed, current information, and review depth.

prompt_templates.title: Specify a Production Audit Cascade
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production-ready cascade for [repository]. Include sandboxed Codex iteration, shared memory keys, GitHub handoffs, rollback points, and human approval gates.

output_examples.input: Create a cascade for a complete development workflow.
output: A staged workflow that researches best practices, prototypes changes, tests in a sandbox, generates diagrams, runs style review, and prepares a pull request.
Each stage includes the preferred model, expected inputs, outputs, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: Design a bug-fix workflow with root cause analysis.
output: A cascade that starts with root cause analysis, chooses a simple or complex branch, tests the fix, checks regressions, and documents the result.
The workflow includes escalation when automated repair fails.

output_examples.input: Coordinate several quality checks at once.
output: A parallel quality workflow that assigns independent checks to separate workers and merges results into one report.
The final stage highlights blockers, warnings, and recommended next actions.

best_practices: Use explicit inputs, outputs, and approval gates for every stage that can change files or external systems.
Run Codex auto-fix stages in an isolated sandbox with a strict iteration limit and regression checks.
Keep model routing rules simple enough that users can predict why each model was selected.
anti_patterns: Do not enable full-auto repair on production branches without review and rollback controls.
Do not store sensitive repository data in shared or global memory unless the retention policy is clear.
Do not route every task to parallel workers when stages depend on ordered results.

faq.question: Does this skill execute workflows directly?
faq.answer: No. It describes how to design cascade workflows. Execution requires separate tools and permissions.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude Code and Codex together?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents routing patterns that assign stages to Claude, Codex, Claude Code, Gemini, or swarm workers.

faq.question: Is the Codex auto-fix pattern safe?
faq.answer: It can be safe when isolated, limited, and reviewed. Avoid running it with broad write access or no approval gate.

faq.question: What does ruv-swarm add?
faq.answer: It adds a pattern for coordinating parallel workers, shared memory, recovery, and performance tracking.

faq.question: What should I define before using it?
faq.answer: Define the goal, available micro-skills, required models, external tools, safety limits, and review checkpoints.

faq.question: Can it create GitHub pull requests?
faq.answer: The skill includes GitHub handoff patterns, but pull request creation requires a configured GitHub tool and user permission.
